question: [File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getByEvmAddressAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a crafted 20-byte EVM address where the first 4 bytes (read via buffer.getInt()) are zero and the next 8 bytes (buffer.getLong()) are zero, causing the service to resolve the address as a long-form entity num lookup via findById(buffer.getLong()) instead of findByEvmAddress(), thereby redirecting the transaction history of a different account to appear under an attacker-controlled EVM address query?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getByEvmAddressAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user craft an EVM address of the form 0x000000000000000000000000<num_hex> where the first 12 bytes are all zeros, causing getByEvmAddressAndType() to branch into the findById(buffer.getLong()) path and return the transaction history of an arbitrary account identified only by its numeric ID, effectively bypassing the EVM address-to-entity binding and misrepresenting which account's history is shown?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getByEvmAddressAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user submit two different EVM address strings — one a true 20-byte EVM address and one a long-form zero-prefix alias — that both resolve to the same entity via different code paths (findByEvmAddress vs findById), causing inconsistent transaction history attribution depending on which lookup path is triggered, and allowing an attacker to present one account's history as belonging to a different address identity?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getByAliasAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a Base32-encoded alias string that, after decodeBase32() decoding, produces the same byte sequence as a different account's alias stored in the entity table, causing findByAlias() to return that account's entity and its associated transaction history, effectively reorganizing which account's history is attributed to which alias?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getByAliasAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit Base32 decoding leniency in decodeBase32() — such as padding normalization, case folding, or ignored characters — to produce multiple distinct alias strings that all decode to the same byte array, causing the same account's transaction history to be retrievable under multiple alias identities and confusing audit trails about which alias was used to access the history?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getByIdAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an EntityIdInput with shard=0, realm=0, and a large num value that, after EntityId.of(shard, realm, num) encoding, produces the same encoded long ID as a different entity with non-zero shard/realm, causing getByIdAndType() to return that entity's transaction history under a misrepresented shard.realm.num identity?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getByIdAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the EntityId bit-packing scheme (10-bit shard, 16-bit realm, 38-bit num) to craft a shard.realm.num triple that encodes to the same 64-bit long as another entity's ID, causing findById(entityId.getId()) to return a different entity's transaction history than the one nominally identified by the supplied shard.realm.num?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getByIdAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user pass an EntityIdInput where shard and realm are both zero and num equals a raw encoded ID of a high-shard entity (e.g., num=180146733873889290 for 10.10.10), causing EntityId.of(0,0,num) to encode to a different long than EntityId.of(10,10,10), yet if the @Min(value:0) directive on num accepts large Long values, potentially causing unexpected entity resolution and misattribution of transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getByEvmAddressAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user submit an EVM address without the '0x' prefix that, after Strings.CS.removeStart() in decodeEvmAddress(), is treated identically to one with the prefix, and then craft the hex string to match a long-form zero-prefix pattern, causing the ByteBuffer branch to resolve to a different entity's ID and return that entity's transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getByEvmAddressAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an EVM address where bytes 0-3 (getInt()) are zero and bytes 4-11 (getLong()) are zero but bytes 12-19 (the final getLong()) encode a valid entity num, causing the service to call findById() with that num and return the transaction history of the entity at that numeric ID, even if that entity has a completely different EVM address registered in the database?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getByEvmAddressAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user craft an EVM address that passes the @Pattern(regexp: '^(0x)?[a-fA-F0-9]{40}$') validation but whose decoded bytes trigger the long-form path (first 12 bytes zero) to resolve to entity num=0, causing findById(0) to return EntityId.EMPTY or an unexpected entity, potentially misrepresenting the transaction history of the zero entity?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getByAliasAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a Base32 alias that decodes to an empty byte array (e.g., an empty or padding-only string), causing findByAlias(new byte[0]) to match an entity whose alias column is an empty byte array, and returning that entity's transaction history under a misleading alias identity?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getByAliasAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that findByAlias() uses a native SQL query ('select * from entity where alias = ?1 and deleted is not true') with a byte array parameter, and supply a specially crafted Base32 alias that decodes to bytes matching a different account's alias, causing that account's full entity state and transaction history to be returned under the attacker's alias query?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getByIdAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user use the 'id' field (Node.id, an opaque Base64-encoded GraphQL ID) in AccountInput to supply a crafted Base64 string that, after getId() decoding and splitting by ':', resolves to a different entity's shard.realm.num than what the user nominally claims, causing the transaction history of that other entity to be returned?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getByIdAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a Node.id (Base64-encoded opaque ID) where the class name prefix (parts.get(0)) matches 'Account' but the remaining parts encode a different entity's numeric ID, causing the service to return that entity's transaction history while the GraphQL response presents it as the account identified by the opaque ID?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getByEvmAddressAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the ByteBuffer.wrap() parsing in getByEvmAddressAndType() by supplying an EVM address whose decoded byte array is fewer than 20 bytes (e.g., due to a decoding edge case in Hex.decodeHex()), causing a BufferUnderflowException or incorrect integer/long reads that misidentify the target entity and return wrong transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getByEvmAddressAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an EVM address with mixed-case hex characters (e.g., EIP-55 checksum address) that passes the @Pattern regex but, after Hex.decodeHex() normalization, resolves to a different entity than the one the user intended, causing transaction history misattribution between two accounts that share similar hex representations?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getByIdAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the EntityType filter (filter(e -> e.getType() == type)) in getByIdAndType() by querying an entity ID that belongs to a CONTRACT type entity using the ACCOUNT type query, causing the filter to return empty and hiding the contract's transaction history, effectively reorganizing what history is visible for that entity ID?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getByAliasAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the EntityType filter in getByAliasAndType() (filter(e -> e.getType() == type)) to probe whether a given alias belongs to an ACCOUNT vs CONTRACT vs TOKEN entity, thereby mapping the entity type landscape and using that information to reorganize their understanding of which transaction history belongs to which entity type?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getByEvmAddressAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an EVM address that resolves via the long-form path (first 12 bytes zero) to an entity num that corresponds to a deleted entity (deleted=true), and since findById() does NOT filter on deleted status (unlike findByAlias and findByEvmAddress which use 'deleted is not true'), retrieve the transaction history of a deleted account that should no longer be accessible?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getByIdAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user use getByIdAndType() (which calls findById() without a 'deleted is not true' filter) to retrieve the entity state and associated transaction history of a deleted account, while getByAliasAndType() and getByEvmAddressAndType() (via findByEvmAddress) would return empty for the same entity, creating an inconsistency in transaction history visibility that an attacker can exploit to access history of supposedly-deleted accounts?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getByEvmAddressAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the asymmetry between the long-form EVM address path (findById, no deleted filter) and the native EVM address path (findByEvmAddress, has 'deleted is not true' filter) to retrieve transaction history of a deleted entity by encoding its numeric ID as a zero-prefix EVM address, bypassing the deletion filter that would otherwise hide the entity?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getByAliasAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a Base32 alias string that, due to the Apache Commons Codec Base32 decoder's handling of non-canonical encodings (e.g., extra padding, non-standard characters), decodes to a byte sequence that matches a different account's alias than the canonical encoding of that alias would produce, causing the wrong account's transaction history to be returned?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getByEvmAddressAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user send a high volume of distinct EVM address queries that all trigger the long-form path (first 12 bytes zero) with different entity nums, causing the CachedPreparsedDocumentProvider to cache the parsed query document and serve it for all subsequent requests, while the actual entity resolution varies per request, creating a scenario where cached query metadata is decoupled from the actual entity whose history is returned?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/service/EntityService.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getByIdAndType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the EntityId cache (Caffeine cache keyed by encoded long ID) in EntityId.of(long id) to cause a previously cached EntityId object to be returned for a new query with a different shard.realm.num that encodes to the same long, causing the transaction history of the cached entity to be associated with the new query's identity?
